reason: Both answers converge on the same core judo: refuse the incumbent's checklist axis, weaponize their breadth as bloat/cost, pick a wedge/metric to dominate, get ahead of the RFP by publishing your own evaluation criteria, and shift the buyer from procurement to bottom-up developers. Neither has factual errors, and both are concise and actionable. B adds two extra useful bits — reframing 'safe choice' risk against the incumbent, and an honest caveat that if you can't find a real 10x wedge it's a product problem, not a positioning one — which sharpen the practical advice. However, B opens with a leftover 'Sketch:' outline before the actual answer, which is unpolished clutter for a busy reader and adds no value beyond what the numbered list already covers. A is cleaner and equally complete, hitting every key point (reframe category, weaponize breadth, force your own dominant metric, shape the RFP early, target underserved buyer) without the stray scaffolding. These roughly offset — B's added insights vs. A's cleaner presentation — so on balance the two answers are comparable in quality.
